_meta.keyKnowledge: We are learning to critically analyse statistical claims in media by examining displays, statistics and sampling methods.

_source.w: We are learning to critically analyse statistical claims in media by examining displays, statistics and sampling methods.
y: Critical analysis of statistical claims is one of the most important skills for informed citizenship in a data-driven world.
Understanding how statistics can be used to mislead — through selective reporting, misleading graphs or biased samples — protects us from manipulation.
These skills are essential for evaluating health advice, political claims, advertising and news reporting.
eg: Identifying a misleading graph in a news article that uses a truncated axis to exaggerate differences.
Evaluating a claim: '80% of doctors recommend...' by questioning the sample, methodology and potential bias.
Analysing how changing the scale or type of graph can change the impression data gives.
Identifying cherry-picked statistics that present a partial picture of the full data.
Evaluating: 'A study found a correlation between X and Y. Does this mean X causes Y?'
Writing a critical analysis of a statistical claim from a current media article.
